title: The Civilian Health and Medical Services, Volume II
pages_or_sections: Hong Kong chapter, PDF p. 152 and pp. 174-175; Singapore chapter, PDF pp. 227-229
strengths: Official synthesis with unusually clear civilian battle-window language and explicit separation, in Singapore, between battle-period civilian deaths and later post-surrender executions.
limitations: Hong Kong military fatality line blends killed, missing, believed killed, and died of wounds; civilian attacker side is not always explicitly assigned.
notes: Most important source for Hong Kong and Singapore civilian numerators and civilian denominators.

title: The Battle of Hong Kong (appendix and order of battle)
source_type: official military history / order of battle
strengths: Provides unit-by-unit garrison strengths and official Canadian battle-fatality detail.
limitations: Does not by itself resolve full garrison killed-vs-missing ambiguity for all Commonwealth units.
notes: Best direct denominator source for the defending garrison.

title: The Defence of Hong Kong: December 1941
geographic_scope: Hong Kong and South China theater
strengths: Good operational summary of the attacking force, including explicit battalion-level assault structure and broader South China force context.
limitations: Does not provide a clean final Japanese headcount for everyone directly exposed in the Hong Kong episode.
notes: Used to build a transparent modeled Japanese force range.

title: Fighting It Out: Canadian Troops at Hong Kong and in Memory
strengths: Useful because it quotes separate killed and missing figures for the garrison, citing the Canadian official history.
limitations: Secondary synthesis rather than the original official-history page image; still leaves unresolved how many 'missing' should be treated as direct battle deaths.
notes: Used mainly to keep killed and missing distinct instead of silently collapsing them.

title: Hong Kong's Civilian Fatalities of the Second World War
strengths: Specialized modern work on Hong Kong civilian fatalities; valuable as a higher alternative benchmark.
limitations: Only a preview/snippet was accessible in this pass, so it is used only as an upper-bound cross-check, not a primary numerator source.
notes: The snippet references Selwyn Selwyn-Clarke and '1941: Deaths 4,000'.

title: The Japanese Thrust
author_or_org: Lionel Wigmore / Australian War Memorial official history
pages_or_sections: Section on fall of Singapore; lines around capture of island and campaign loss totals
strengths: Official military history with a clean Japanese killed/wounded line for the capture of Singapore Island and a separate campaign-wide casualty context.
limitations: Still does not give a clean direct-death figure for all defenders on the island or for the full city-under-attack civilian window.
notes: Useful for explaining why Singapore is not yet fully estimated.

title: Remembering the fall of Singapore
strengths: Provides a conservative lower bound showing that at least 880 Australian deaths occurred in the single week of island fighting.
limitations: Nationality-specific and not a full Allied defender death series.
notes: Good evidence that island-battle fatalities were substantial, but insufficient for a full defender numerator.

title: The Nanjing Incident: An Examination of the Civilian Population
pages_or_sections: Population estimates; discussion of post-fall executions
strengths: Best source found for Nanjing civilian denominator reconstruction and for warning against simplistic use of execution totals as civilian battle deaths.
limitations: Does not solve the core problem of isolating battle-phase civilian direct deaths inside the 9-13 December window.
notes: Critical for keeping battle and massacre phases separate.

title: Defending Nanking: An Examination of the Capital Garrison Forces
strengths: Strong on force sizes and Japanese losses, with explicit discussion of the battle's periodization and spatial definition.
limitations: Again, strongest on military rather than civilian numerators.
notes: Main military denominator source for any future Nanjing estimate.

title: August 1937: War and the Death en masse of Civilians
pages_or_sections: First three weeks of fighting; refugee outflow
geographic_scope: Shanghai urban districts and surrounding towns/villages
strengths: Strong on civilian death and refugee-flight evidence for the initial urban phase.
limitations: Source is phase- and district-specific; not enough by itself to support a whole-episode DR for the entire August-November campaign.
notes: Useful for shortlist scoping, not yet enough for a full estimate.
